content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Honorable Bill Bradley 
Assemblyman, 76th District 
state Capitol 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
/ 
Dear Assemblyman Bradley: 
February 3, 1987 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-87-020 
You have requested advice regarding your duties under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act") • .Y 
QUESTION 
Are you required to disqualify yourself from participating 
in decisions involving AB 60 (Elder) of 1987? 
CONCLUSION 
You are not required by the Act to disqualify yourself from 
participating in decisions on AB 60. 
FACTS 
You are a member of the California State Assembly. 
You are 
over 55 years of age and considering selling your present home. 
AB 60 provides that a property owner over 55 years of age 
may keep the current property tax assessment on his or her home 
if it is sold and a new home in the same county of equal or 
lesser value is purchased • 
.Y Government Code Sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California 
Administrative Code Section 18000, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California 
Administrative Code. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


Honorable Bill Bradley 
February 3, 1987 
Page 2 
ANALYSIS 
section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, 
participating in, or attempting to influence a governmental 
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest. 
Section 87100 applies to state 
legislators, who are public officials within the meaning of 
Section 87100.~ (Sections 82048, 82049.) 
A public official has a financial interest in a decision if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her 
immediate family or on, among other things: 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth one 
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 
section 87103{b). 
Thus, you are required to disqualify yourself from 
participating in a particular decision if the decision would 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your 
residence, distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally. 
Disqualification would be required even if you 
would not personally benefit from the transaction. 
Regulation 18703 (copy enclosed) provides that a material 
financial effect on an official's interest is distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally unless the decision 
will affect the official's interest in substantially the same 
manner as it will affect all members of the public or a 
significant segment of the public. 
The population of property 
owners over 55 years of age, who would be affected by AB 60, 
represents a significant segment of the public. 
The particular facts of each decision you face will be 
determinative of the disqualification issue. 
However, you are 
not required to disqualify yourself unless the particular 
decision you face will affect you or a segment of the 
£I However, the remedies provided in the Act for 
violations of section 87100 are not applicable to elected state 
officers, including state legislators. 
(Section 87102.) 


Honorable Bill Bradley 
February 3, 1987 
Page 3 
population smaller than the population of property owners over 
55 years of age differently than other members of the public. 
If AB 60 is amended in any significant respect, you would be 
wise to contact us again for advice. 
If you have any questions concerning this letter, you may 
contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
DMG:plh 
Enclosure 
·u=~ fL{fjL 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 


January 5, 1986 
John Larsen, Chairman 
Political Pract 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
Dear ~1r. Larsen: 
Commission 
With 
passage of Propos 
60 on the ballot in November, 
slat 
has been introduced providing 
a property owner 
over 55 
of age may keep the same property tax assessment 
his or her new home in the same county is of equal or lesser 
value that the home b 
sold. The bill number is AB 60, 
My question is, would it be a conflict of interest for me to 
vote 
this legislation s 
I am a legislator in 
t age 
bracket and am interested in sell 
my present home. 
Please advise. 
Sincerely, 
!3~ bA4-7 
BILL BRADLEY 
sernb 
,76th District 
:b 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Honorable Bill Bradley 
Assemblyman, 76th District 
state Capitol 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Assemblyman Bradley: 
January 9, 1987 
Re: 
87-020 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on January 7, 1987 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days. 
You also should be aware that your 
letter and our response are public records which may be 
disclosed to the public upon receipt of a proper request for 
disclosure. 
DMG:plh 
Very truly yours, 
'~ 
~t(,"r~ rh 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • 
(916)322~5660

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are you required to disqualify yourself from participating 
in decisions involving AB 60 (Elder) of 1987?

CONCLUSION: You are not required by the Act to disqualify yourself from 
participating in decisions on AB 60.
